text: DL-Alanine (DL-Ala), chemical formula C3H7NO2, molecular weight 89.10, is a racemic mixture crystal of amino acids, consisting of equal parts L- and D-alanine enantiomers. It crystallizes in the non-centrosymmetric, polar orthorhombic space group Pna21 (No. 33), with unit cell a = 12.0263 Å, b = 6.0321 Å, c = 5.829 Å, Z = 4, volume 422.88 Å³, determined by single-crystal X-ray diffraction at 293 K with an R factor of 2.52% (CCDC 164668). Notably, DL-alanine exhibits parallel packing of alternating L and D isomers through head-to-tail sequences and glide-plane interactions, which results in a net dipole polarization along the crystal axes—a property absent in the L-alanine enantiomer due to antiparallel packing that cancels dipoles. In a 2019 density functional theory study, DL-alanine was predicted to exhibit a large longitudinal piezoelectric charge constant e33 of 0.68 C/m², comparable to glycine, with strain constants reaching 18 pm/V and voltage constants exceeding those of inorganic ceramics such as PZT. Experimental measurements on polycrystalline films confirmed an average longitudinal piezoelectric response d33 of 4.0 pC/N (maximum 4.8 pC/N), and maximum open-circuit voltage of 0.8 V under manual compression—twice that of L-alanine or other amino acid crystals. Piezoresponse force microscopy on isolated single crystals yielded an average d33 response of 9.1 pm/V, validating DFT predictions. These results demonstrate that net molecular chirality is not required for piezoelectricity in organic crystals, opening new opportunities for biocompatible piezoelectric energy harvesters.
experimental_method: Piezoelectric measurements (d33 piezometer on films, Piezoresponse Force Microscopy on single crystals)
